reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: All Stacktape config examples use the correct <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> form with TypeScript — no YAML or plain-object violations.
The FeatureComparisonTable for shared vs. dedicated tiers is excellent — concrete, scannable, and decision-useful in a way prose can't match.
FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS-service questions, and choosing/comparison questions — and has 10 well-differentiated entries.
Warning callouts are placed precisely where gotchas live (shared tier VPC restriction, BI Connector CPU impact, auto-scaling shared tier restriction) rather than lumped generically.
When to use / When NOT to use sections are concrete and opinionated with real alternatives named (relational database, DynamoDB).

The connectTo section is clear and grounded: includes a realistic code example, shows the injected env var with the correct resource-name-dependent naming (STP_MAIN_DB_CONNECTION_STRING for mainDb), and follows up with driver usage in a fenced application code block.
Admin credentials section explicitly distinguishes itself from connectTo application access — prevents a common first-time confusion.
Scaling section explains all four auto-scaling knobs (minClusterTier, maxClusterTier, disableDiskScaling, disableScaleDown) with tradeoffs — not just how to set them.
problems: The Provider Configuration section describes a required prerequisite (providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas) with no code example. A first-time user cannot deploy any Atlas cluster without this configuration, but the page only lists the three fields in prose. The 'Basic example' Info callout acknowledges this gap but does not fill it.

The Networking section describes all four accessibility modes in a table but provides no code example showing where or how to write this configuration (providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas.accessibility). Without seeing the config shape, the reader does not know this goes in providerConfig rather than on the cluster resource itself.

The connectTo section claims 'Creates an AWS IAM-authenticated database user with readWriteAnyDatabase access, scoped to the specific cluster.' The source only states 'temporary credential-less access → CONNECTION_STRING' — it does not name the IAM mechanism, role, or scope. This is an unsupported specific claim that the source-grounding verifier is likely to flag.

The shared-tier regional fallback claim — 'Stacktape automatically selects the closest available region' — is not present in the provided source files. The source says nothing about a closest-region fallback, making this an invented fact.
mandatoryFixes: Add a code example to the Provider Configuration section showing providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas with organizationId, publicKey (as $Secret()), and privateKey (as $Secret()) inside a full defineConfig block. Without this, a first-time user cannot deploy — the section describes a required prerequisite but leaves them with no config to copy.

Add a code example to the Networking section showing providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas.accessibility set to one of the non-default modes (e.g., vpc with a whitelistedIps entry) so readers see where in the config this lives. The table of modes is useful but not sufficient — the reader needs to know the property path and shape.

Narrow the connectTo mechanism claim to match the source language. Replace 'Creates an AWS IAM-authenticated database user with readWriteAnyDatabase access, scoped to the specific cluster' with a paraphrase of the source: 'grants your workload credential-less access to the cluster and injects the connection string'. Do not assert specific role names or scoping rules the source does not state.

Remove or qualify the shared-tier regional fallback claim ('Stacktape automatically selects the closest available region'). The source does not describe this behavior. Either remove the sentence or rephrase as 'shared tiers may not be available in all AWS regions — check the Atlas documentation for supported regions'.
optionalImprovements: The 'Basic example' Info callout says provider config is required 'as described in Provider configuration' but does not link to that section anchor. Add a hash-anchor link (#provider-configuration) so readers can jump directly.

Consider showing both the provider config and a cluster resource in one consolidated 'Getting started' example near the top of the page. Right now the Basic example is correct in isolation but misleading — it will fail to deploy without providerConfig. A single end-to-end example would better serve the 'copy-paste and ship' persona.

The Replication section mentions analytics nodes 'don't compete with production reads or writes' but does not reference how they relate to the BI Connector's readPreference: 'analytics' setting. A one-sentence cross-reference would connect the two features.

The Sharding section could note that changing numShards on a deployed cluster is a destructive operation (requires snapshot restore or data migration in Atlas) — an important production gotcha not mentioned anywhere on the page.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Comprehensive section coverage — all source-backed properties (tier table, scaling bounds, replication node types, PITR, BI Connector, sharding, networking modes) are explained with working code examples and clear when-to-use guidance.
The cluster-tier FeatureComparisonTable is precise and scannable; the shared-vs-dedicated split is correctly sourced and the M30+ sharding threshold is accurate.
FAQ mixes all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS/Atlas service questions, comparison questions) with 10 high-quality Q&A pairs that stand alone as extractable snippets.

All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with TypeScript source; no forbidden YAML blocks, no StacktapeConfig typed style, no fenced TypeScript blocks with defineConfig.
Auto-scaling bounds and disk-scaling behavior (75%/1h scale-up, 50%/24h scale-down, 90% disk expansion) are sourced directly from the JSDoc and explained accurately.
Warning callouts are placed where they materially affect configuration (shared tier VPC restriction, BI Connector M10/M20 perf impact, auto-scaling tier requirement).
Single <ApiReference definitionName="MongoDbAtlasClusterProps" /> with a correct definitionName that matches the source type — no extra blocks or PropertiesTable.
problems: FACTUAL — UNSUPPORTED AUTH MECHANISM: The Connecting section claims connectTo 'Creates an AWS IAM-authenticated database user with `readWriteAnyDatabase` access, scoped to the specific cluster' and 'The IAM authentication is handled transparently — your workload's IAM role is granted access.' The source's connectTo JSDoc says only 'temporary credential-less access → CONNECTION_STRING' for MongoDbAtlasCluster. The specific mechanism (IAM, readWriteAnyDatabase role) is not grounded. This same claim is repeated verbatim in the FAQ answer for 'How does my application connect?'.

FACTUAL — UNSUPPORTED ADMIN ROLES: The Admin credentials subsection states the admin user 'is created with `atlasAdmin` and `readWriteAnyDatabase` roles, scoped to the specific cluster.' The source's adminUserCredentials JSDoc says only 'Admin user for direct database access' with no role details. These role names are not in the provided source.

FACTUAL — UNSUPPORTED VPC PEERING CLAIM: The Networking section opens with 'Stacktape automatically sets up VPC peering between the Atlas project and your stack's VPC.' The provided source files contain no reference to VPC peering automation. This is a concrete deployment behavior claim that engineers will act on.

FACTUAL — UNSUPPORTED SHARED TIER REGION FALLBACK: The Cluster tiers section states 'Shared tiers are available in a limited set of AWS regions — if your stack deploys to a region without shared-tier support, Stacktape automatically selects the closest available region.' This behavior is not mentioned in the provided source.

FACTUAL — UNSUPPORTED LAMBDA joinDefaultVpc REQUIREMENT: The Networking section states 'For Lambda functions, this means setting `joinDefaultVpc: true` on the function' for VPC/scoping-workloads-in-vpc mode. This specific property name and requirement is not grounded in the provided source files.
mandatoryFixes: Fix the connectTo mechanism description: replace 'Creates an AWS IAM-authenticated database user with `readWriteAnyDatabase` access' and 'IAM authentication is handled transparently' with wording that matches the source: connectTo grants 'temporary credential-less access' to the cluster and injects CONNECTION_STRING. Do not specify the auth mechanism (IAM, password, role name) unless it appears in the source. Update the duplicate claim in the FAQ answer for 'How does my application connect?'.

Remove or caveat the admin user role claims: 'atlasAdmin and readWriteAnyDatabase roles' is not in the source. Either remove the role names entirely, or prefix with 'typically' and add a note to verify in the Atlas console — do not present it as a Stacktape-documented guarantee.

Remove or caveat the VPC peering sentence: 'Stacktape automatically sets up VPC peering between the Atlas project and your stack's VPC' is not sourced. If VPC-mode networking relies on peering, it should be verified in the runtime source before claiming it; otherwise replace with the source-grounded description that vpc/scoping-workloads-in-vpc modes restrict access to resources in your VPC.

Remove the shared tier region fallback claim ('Stacktape automatically selects the closest available region') — it is not in any provided source file. Either verify it in the runtime source or omit it.

Remove the Lambda `joinDefaultVpc: true` guidance from the Networking section — the specific property and its VPC-mode requirement for MongoDB Atlas is not grounded in the provided source files. Replace with a link to the Lambda function networking page for VPC configuration details.
optionalImprovements: The MongoDbAtlasProvider type (with organizationId, publicKey, privateKey fields) is not in the provided source files. Consider adding a soft caveat ('Refer to the Atlas Console for exact field names') or verifying these three fields in the runtime source before treating them as authoritative.

The Replication section could mention that custom replication requires M10+ explicitly (the source's MongoDbReplication interface is nested under MongoDbAtlasClusterProps without a tier restriction note, but the prose omits this context until the final sentence — move it earlier for production engineers reading top-down).

The Basic example uses `version: '7.0'` but the prose says the version defaults to '7.0' — consider removing the explicit version from the example to reflect the minimal required config (only clusterTier is required per the source).

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The page has a strong resource-page structure: answer-first opening, When to use, When NOT to use, Basic example, decision sections, API reference, Referenceable parameters, and a broad FAQ.
Stacktape config examples use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/> with TypeScript class-based resources and no hand-authored Stacktape YAML.
The tier comparison table is useful and citation-friendly, and most headings are compact noun-form headings.
The page gives practical decision guidance for shared vs dedicated tiers, sharding, BI Connector, backups, and auto-scaling instead of only listing properties.
problems: Several Stacktape-specific technical claims are not grounded in the provided source files. Provider credential field names (`organizationId`, `publicKey`, `privateKey`) are not defined in the supplied `MongoDbAtlasProvider` source, so the Provider configuration section overstates unsupported details.

The `connectTo` section invents behavior beyond the provided source: creating an AWS IAM-authenticated database user, assigning `readWriteAnyDatabase`, and granting the workload IAM role access to the cluster are not stated for MongoDB Atlas in the provided files. The source only supports `MongoDbAtlasCluster` injecting `CONNECTION_STRING` and describes it as temporary credential-less access.

The Admin credentials section claims the admin user is created with `atlasAdmin` and `readWriteAnyDatabase` roles, but `MongoDbAdminUserCredentials` only contains `userName` and `password`; roles are unsupported by the provided source.

The Networking section says Stacktape automatically sets up VPC peering between the Atlas project and the stack VPC, but the provided source only defines accessibility modes and whitelisted IP behavior. VPC peering creation is not grounded here.

The Networking section claims shared tiers cannot use `vpc` or `scoping-workloads-in-vpc`, but the supplied type comments do not state this constraint. The tier table also marks VPC network access mode as dedicated-only without direct support in source.

The Basic example says `version` defaults to `"7.0"` and calls it MongoDB's current stable release. The default is sourced, but "current stable release" is time-sensitive and not grounded in the provided source.

The page sets and discusses many tunable values well, but the Basic example’s M10 choice could better explain that M2/M5 are also valid and cheaper; otherwise the first copyable example may imply dedicated M10 is the minimum practical starting point.

Some broad comparison claims about DynamoDB and relational databases go beyond the supplied source list. They may be useful, but should be phrased as general decision guidance and linked rather than as Stacktape-specific facts unless those resource sources are available.
mandatoryFixes: Replace unsupported provider credential specifics with narrower wording grounded in `_root.d.ts`: MongoDB Atlas requires `providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas`; only document `accessibility` details that are visible in the provided source unless the provider type is added to the source list.

Rewrite the `connectTo` bullets to the sourced facts: `connectTo` injects `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_CONNECTION_STRING` for `MongoDbAtlasCluster`, and the source describes MongoDB Atlas access as temporary credential-less access. Remove or source the IAM-authenticated database user, `readWriteAnyDatabase`, and workload IAM-role access claims.

Remove or source the admin-user role claims. Based on the provided source, say only that `adminUserCredentials` creates an admin user for direct database access and requires `userName` and `password`.
Remove or source the VPC peering claim. Keep the four `providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas.accessibility` modes and `whitelistedIps` behavior, because those are defined in `MongoDbAtlasAccessibility`.

Remove or source the claim that shared tiers cannot use `vpc` or `scoping-workloads-in-vpc`. Do not present it as a hard Stacktape constraint unless the runtime validator or provider source confirms it.
Change "MongoDB's current stable release" to a sourced statement such as "Stacktape defaults `version` to `7.0`; supported values are `5.0`, `6.0`, and `7.0`."
optionalImprovements: Add a provider configuration CodeBlock only if the `MongoDbAtlasProvider` type source is available; otherwise keep the section conceptual and avoid field-level claims.
In the Basic example, consider using M2 for the cheapest deployable example or explicitly say the example uses M10 because dedicated tiers unlock auto-scaling and backups.
Add one sentence in Backups noting that the provided source does not expose retention customization, so readers should use the API reference rather than assuming custom retention is available.
Tighten FAQ answers that repeat full section content; keep the broad FAQ, but make repeated answers shorter and link back to the relevant H2 sections.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is strong on supported cluster properties, pricing, tiers, scaling, backups, sharding, referenceable params, and TypeScript CodeBlock usage. The main publication risks are unsupported Stacktape-specific claims around providerConfig shape, connectTo mechanics, networking implementation, admin-user roles, and a few Atlas behavior details stated more strongly than the provided types support.

issues.statement: “The provider configuration has three credential fields: organizationId, publicKey, privateKey” and “MongoDB Atlas clusters require ... API credentials configured in providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas (organization ID, public key, private key).”
issues.reason: The provided source confirms that providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas exists, but it does not include the MongoDbAtlasProvider type or these field names. These are Stacktape-specific configuration property names; without the provider type in the source list, the page cannot document them as factual fields.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the section to the sourced fact: MongoDB Atlas requires providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas for third-party credentials/settings. Do not name organizationId, publicKey, privateKey unless the MongoDbAtlasProvider source is included.

evidence.quote:
#### Credentials and settings for 3rd-party services (MongoDB Atlas, Upstash).
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Required only if you use `mongo-db-atlas-cluster` or `upstash-redis` resources in your stack.

issues.statement: “Adding the cluster's resource name to a workload's connectTo list automatically creates an AWS IAM-authenticated database user with readWriteAnyDatabase access, scoped to the specific cluster” and “your workload's IAM role is granted access to the cluster.”
issues.reason: The connectTo JSDoc for MongoDbAtlasCluster only documents “temporary credential-less access” and injection of CONNECTION_STRING. The source does not support the specific implementation details about creating an AWS IAM-authenticated database user, readWriteAnyDatabase role, cluster scoping, or workload IAM role grants.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with the sourced wording: connectTo gives the consuming workload temporary credential-less access and injects STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_CONNECTION_STRING. Avoid naming IAM database users, Atlas roles, or workload IAM role behavior unless runtime source confirms it.

evidence.quote: **`MongoDbAtlasCluster`** — temporary credential-less access → `CONNECTION_STRING`
Injects environment variables with connection details: `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]`

issues.statement: “The admin user is created with atlasAdmin and readWriteAnyDatabase roles, scoped to the specific cluster.”
issues.reason: The adminUserCredentials source only says it creates an admin user for direct database access and defines userName/password. It does not document Atlas roles, readWriteAnyDatabase, or cluster scoping.
issues.suggestedFix: State only that adminUserCredentials creates an admin user for direct database access and is not required for app-to-database access via connectTo.

evidence.quote:
#### Admin user for direct database access (e.g., from your local machine or admin tools).
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Not required for app-to-database access via `connectTo` — that's handled automatically.

issues.statement: “Stacktape automatically sets up VPC peering between the Atlas project and your stack's VPC.”
issues.reason: The provided sources document MongoDB Atlas accessibility modes and VPC-based access, but they do not state that Stacktape automatically creates VPC peering between Atlas and the stack VPC. This is Stacktape-specific networking behavior that would affect production configuration.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the VPC peering claim or replace it with source-grounded wording: accessibilityMode can be internet, vpc, scoping-workloads-in-vpc, or whitelisted-ips-only; vpc modes restrict access to resources in your VPC plus whitelisted IPs.

evidence.quote: accessibilityMode: 'internet' | 'vpc' | 'scoping-workloads-in-vpc' | 'whitelisted-ips-only';

- **`vpc`**: Only from resources in your VPC + any `whitelistedIps`.
   * - **`scoping-workloads-in-vpc`**: Like `vpc`, but also requires security-group access via `connectTo`.

issues.statement: “The provider also accepts an accessibility property that controls network access for all MongoDB Atlas clusters in the stack” and “Network access ... is configured at the provider level through providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas.accessibility.”
issues.reason: The source defines MongoDbAtlasAccessibility and its fields, but the provided source does not show the MongoDbAtlasProvider shape or prove that the property is named accessibility under providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas. The “all clusters in the stack” scope is also not shown in the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Write cautiously: “MongoDB Atlas accessibility is represented by MongoDbAtlasAccessibility with modes...” Avoid the exact providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas.accessibility path and stack-wide scope unless MongoDbAtlasProvider source is available.

evidence.quote: mongoDbAtlas?: MongoDbAtlasProvider;

issues.statement: “Shared tiers are available in a limited set of AWS regions — if your stack deploys to a region without shared-tier support, Stacktape automatically selects the closest available region.”
issues.reason: The provided MongoDB Atlas cluster type documents shared tiers and tier constraints, but it does not mention regional availability or automatic closest-region selection. This is Stacktape-specific deployment behavior and should not be asserted from the provided sources.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the automatic closest-region selection claim unless runtime source confirms it. Keep shared-tier guidance to sourced limitations such as M2/M5 being shared and not supporting diskSizeGB, auto-scaling, configurable backups, PITR, sharding, or VPC modes where directly sourced.

evidence.quote: Instance size. M2/M5 = shared (cheapest). M10+ = dedicated (more features, auto-scaling, backups).
Disk size in GB. Not available for shared tiers (M2/M5). M10+ auto-scales storage by default.

issues.statement: “The version property defaults to "7.0" — MongoDB's current stable release” and FAQ repeats “The default is 7.0, which is MongoDB's current stable release.”
issues.reason: The source supports the Stacktape default and allowed values, but it does not support the external claim that MongoDB 7.0 is the current stable release. That phrase is time-sensitive and may become stale even if Stacktape’s default remains 7.0.
issues.suggestedFix: Say “The version property defaults to '7.0'. Supported versions are '5.0', '6.0', and '7.0'.”

evidence.quote: @default "7.0"

issues.statement: “Using 'analytics' is recommended to route BI queries to dedicated analytics nodes” and “configure analytics nodes in replication.numAnalyticsNodes and set readPreference: 'analytics'.”
issues.reason: The source says readPreference can be primary, secondary, or analytics, and says to use analytics to avoid impacting production queries. It does not state that setting readPreference: 'analytics' requires or automatically uses configured dedicated analytics nodes. The draft should avoid implying a validated relationship between the BI Connector setting and replication.numAnalyticsNodes beyond what is documented.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the sourced advice that readPreference controls the node type and that analytics can avoid impacting production queries. Phrase the analytics-node recommendation cautiously, for example: “If you configure analytics nodes, use readPreference: 'analytics' for BI workloads.”

evidence.quote: readPreference?: 'primary' | 'secondary' | 'analytics';
Which node type the BI Connector reads from. Use `analytics` to avoid impacting production queries.
numAnalyticsNodes?: number;

positiveFindings: All Stacktape configuration examples use <CodeBlock intellisense> with a TypeScript tab and defineConfig/class-based resources; no hand-authored YAML Stacktape config appears.
The API reference uses the correct Props-suffixed definition name: MongoDbAtlasClusterProps.

The draft correctly documents sourced core fields: clusterTier is required; version supports 5.0, 6.0, and 7.0 with default 7.0; numShards defaults to 1 and requires M30+ for sharded mode; enablePointInTimeRecovery requires enableBackups and M10+; MongoDbAtlasCluster exposes connectionString as a referenceable parameter.
The connectTo environment variable example correctly derives STP_MAIN_DB_CONNECTION_STRING from the resource name mainDb.
The page includes a pricing context near the top using prices present in the MongoDB Atlas cluster type JSDoc.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the MongoDB Atlas type surface, but it makes several Stacktape-specific claims that are not grounded in the provided sources. The biggest risks are unsupported provider credential fields, unsupported claims about IAM/database-user behavior for `connectTo`, and one incorrect interpretation of analytics nodes.

issues.statement: The provider configuration has three credential fields: `organizationId`, `publicKey`, and `privateKey`.
issues.reason: The provided source only shows that `providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas` exists and is required for MongoDB Atlas resources; it does not provide the `MongoDbAtlasProvider` shape or these field names. These are Stacktape configuration property names, so they should not be documented without source backing.
issues.suggestedFix: Either provide source for `MongoDbAtlasProvider` before publishing this section, or narrow the prose to: MongoDB Atlas resources require `providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas` credentials, without naming exact fields.

issues.statement: Adding the cluster to `connectTo` creates an AWS IAM-authenticated database user with `readWriteAnyDatabase` access, scoped to the specific cluster; IAM authentication is handled transparently and the workload role is granted access to the cluster.
issues.reason: The source documents `connectTo` for MongoDB Atlas only as temporary credential-less access plus the `CONNECTION_STRING` environment variable. It does not mention creating an Atlas database user, `readWriteAnyDatabase`, IAM authentication, or workload-role permissions for MongoDB Atlas.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with the sourced behavior: `connectTo` injects `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_CONNECTION_STRING` for `MongoDbAtlasCluster` and provides temporary credential-less access. Avoid naming Atlas roles or IAM mechanics unless source is added.

evidence.quote: **`MongoDbAtlasCluster`** — temporary credential-less access → `CONNECTION_STRING`

issues.statement: Analytics nodes are isolated nodes for long-running analytical queries. Queries on analytics nodes don't compete with production reads or writes.
issues.reason: The draft assigns the analytics-node behavior to `numAnalyticsNodes`, but the source description for `numAnalyticsNodes` says “Read-only nodes for long-running analytics queries,” while the separate `readPreference: 'analytics'` guidance says that setting is used to avoid impacting production queries. The draft overstates isolation and attaches the stronger behavior to the wrong property.
issues.suggestedFix: Say `numAnalyticsNodes` configures read-only nodes for long-running analytics queries, and separately say the BI Connector `readPreference: 'analytics'` is intended to avoid impacting production queries.

evidence.quote: #### Read-only nodes for long-running analytics queries. Prevents impact on primary workload performance.
#### Which node type the BI Connector reads from. Use `analytics` to avoid impacting production queries.

issues.statement: The provider also accepts an `accessibility` property that controls network access for all MongoDB Atlas clusters in the stack.
issues.reason: The source defines `MongoDbAtlasAccessibility`, but the provided source does not show the `MongoDbAtlasProvider` interface or prove that the provider has an `accessibility` property. The “all clusters in the stack” scope is also not stated in the provided type comments.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the access-mode descriptions grounded to `MongoDbAtlasAccessibility`, but avoid saying the property exists at `providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas.accessibility` or that it applies to all clusters unless the provider type source is available.

issues.statement: Stacktape automatically sets up VPC peering between the Atlas project and your stack's VPC.
issues.reason: The provided source documents the `vpc` and `scoping-workloads-in-vpc` accessibility modes, but it does not say Stacktape creates VPC peering. This is Stacktape-specific provisioning behavior and should be sourced before it is stated.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the VPC peering sentence or replace it with the narrower source-backed claim that `vpc` mode limits access to resources in your VPC plus any `whitelistedIps`.

evidence.quote:
- **`vpc`**: Only from resources in your VPC + any `whitelistedIps`.
   * - **`scoping-workloads-in-vpc`**: Like `vpc`, but also requires security-group access via `connectTo`.

issues.statement: If your stack deploys to a region without shared-tier support, Stacktape automatically selects the closest available region.
issues.reason: The source says shared tiers are the cheapest and distinguishes shared versus dedicated tiers, but it does not mention regional availability or automatic closest-region selection. This is a Stacktape-specific default/behavior that affects deployment expectations.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the automatic closest-region sentence unless runtime validator or provisioning source confirms it. A safe version is: “Shared-tier regional availability is not documented in this type layer.”

evidence.quote: #### Instance size. M2/M5 = shared (cheapest). M10+ = dedicated (more features, auto-scaling, backups).

issues.statement: Choose a dedicated tier (M10+) when you need performance isolation; dedicated tiers run on isolated infrastructure with full control over compute and storage.
issues.reason: The source supports that M10+ tiers are dedicated and have more features, auto-scaling, and backups. It does not mention performance isolation, isolated infrastructure, or full control over compute and storage. These are likely Atlas concepts, but they are not grounded in the provided Stacktape sources.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow to source-backed wording: “Choose a dedicated tier (M10+) when you need features such as auto-scaling, configurable backups, disk sizing, PITR, sharding, BI Connector, or custom replication.”

evidence.quote: #### Instance size. M2/M5 = shared (cheapest). M10+ = dedicated (more features, auto-scaling, backups).
#### Disk size in GB. Not available for shared tiers (M2/M5). M10+ auto-scales storage by default.

issues.statement: `whitelistedIps` can be used in any mode except `internet`.
issues.reason: The source says `whitelistedIps` has no effect in `internet` mode, and explains behavior in `vpc`, `scoping-workloads-in-vpc`, and `whitelisted-ips-only`. “Can be used in any mode except internet” implies a validation constraint that is not present in the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the source wording: `whitelistedIps` has no effect in `internet` mode; in `vpc` and `scoping-workloads-in-vpc` it adds external IP access, and in `whitelisted-ips-only` it defines the only allowed IPs.

evidence.quote:
No effect in `internet` mode. In `vpc`/`scoping-workloads-in-vpc`, adds access for IPs outside the VPC.
   * In `whitelisted-ips-only`, these are the only IPs that can connect.

positiveFindings: The page uses class-based `defineConfig` examples inside `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a visible `TypeScript` tab, and it does not hand-author Stacktape YAML examples.
The `<ApiReference definitionName="MongoDbAtlasClusterProps" />` value matches the resource props type in the provided source.
The pricing context near the top is grounded in the MongoDB Atlas cluster JSDoc: M2 starts around $9/month and M10 around $57/month.

The documented `clusterTier`, `version`, `numShards`, backups, PITR, BI Connector, auto-scaling, admin credentials, and referenceable `connectionString` parameters largely match the provided type definitions.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the main MongoDbAtlasClusterProps surface well, including clusterTier, version, scaling, replication, backups, sharding, BI Connector, admin credentials, providerConfig, connectTo, referenceable params, and the API reference. The main API-completeness gap is exact union coverage for tier values, and there are a few demonstrativeness/source-scope risks around examples and provider/accessibility wording.

issues.statement: The draft documents `clusterTier`, `autoScaling.minClusterTier`, and `autoScaling.maxClusterTier`, but does not name every supported tier value in the source union.
issues.reason: Because the page discusses tier selection as a core decision, readers need a compact supported-values anchor. The draft names M2/M5, M10+, M30+, and examples of M40 variants, but omits many exact supported values such as M20, M50, M60, M80, M100, M140, M200, M300, M400, and M700 variants.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a compact tier values table or sentence near “Cluster tiers” that names all supported `clusterTier` values, and note that auto-scaling bounds use the dedicated-tier subset exposed by `minClusterTier` and `maxClusterTier`.

issues.statement: The “Networking” section describes `providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas.accessibility` and its modes, but gives no Stacktape configuration example showing where that provider-level property lives.
issues.reason: The section is about a provider-level configuration shape, not a MongoDbAtlasClusterProps property. A reader could reasonably miss that `accessibility` is outside the cluster resource, especially because all nearby examples instantiate only `MongoDbAtlasCluster`.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a small `<CodeBlock intellisense>` example that returns both `providerConfig: { mongoDbAtlas: { ... accessibility: { accessibilityMode: 'vpc' } } }` and `resources: { mainDb }`, with secrets for credentials. Keep the example focused on the provider-level placement.

evidence.quote:
interface MongoDbAtlasAccessibility {
  /**
   * #### Network access mode.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * - **`internet`**: Accessible from anywhere (credentials still required).
   * - **`vpc`**: Only from resources in your VPC + any `whitelistedIps`.
   * - **`scoping-workloads-in-vpc`**: Like `vpc`, but also requires security-group access via `connectTo`.
   * - **`whitelisted-ips-only`**: Only from IP addresses listed in `whitelistedIps`.
   *
   * @default internet
   */
  accessibilityMode: 'internet' | 'vpc' | 'scoping-workloads-in-vpc' | 'whitelisted-ips-only';

issues.statement: The “Connecting to other resources” example demonstrates `connectTo` but only uses a Lambda function outside the VPC, while the Networking section later says VPC access requires `joinDefaultVpc: true` for Lambda functions.
issues.reason: The example is fine for default `internet` access, but the page also recommends VPC-style access modes for production. Without a caveat, readers may combine the `connectTo` example with `vpc` or `scoping-workloads-in-vpc` accessibility and miss the Lambda VPC requirement stated later.
issues.suggestedFix: After the `connectTo` example, add a short caveat: for default `internet` accessibility, the example is enough; for `vpc` or `scoping-workloads-in-vpc`, Lambda functions also need `joinDefaultVpc: true`. Optionally show that property in the Networking example instead of the basic connection example.

evidence.quote:
- **`vpc`**: Only from resources in your VPC + any `whitelistedIps`.
   * - **`scoping-workloads-in-vpc`**: Like `vpc`, but also requires security-group access via `connectTo`.

When using `vpc` or `scoping-workloads-in-vpc` mode, workloads that connect to the cluster must be able to reach the VPC. For [Lambda functions](/resources/compute/lambda-function), this means setting `joinDefaultVpc: true` on the function.

issues.statement: The draft states that the provider configuration has three credential fields: `organizationId`, `publicKey`, and `privateKey`.
issues.reason: Those fields may be correct in the real codebase, but they are not present in the provided source files for this audit. The only provided root source says `providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas` exists and references `MongoDbAtlasProvider`; the shape of `MongoDbAtlasProvider` is not included.
issues.suggestedFix: Either add the MongoDbAtlasProvider source to the page source set, or narrow the wording to “configure Atlas provider credentials in `providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas`” and avoid enumerating fields unless the provider type is available to the verifier.

evidence.quote:
Credentials and settings for 3rd-party services (MongoDB Atlas, Upstash).
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Required only if you use `mongo-db-atlas-cluster` or `upstash-redis` resources in your stack.

issues.statement: The “BI Connector” section explains `readPreference`, but does not include a demonstrative config example for `biConnector.enabled` and `biConnector.readPreference`.
issues.reason: This section introduces an opt-in feature with a nested object and a union value. The prose names the fields, but the page pattern uses demonstrative examples for other opt-ins like scaling, backups, replication, and sharding; BI Connector is the one comparable feature without an example.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a focused `<CodeBlock intellisense>` example under “BI Connector” showing `biConnector: { enabled: true, readPreference: 'analytics' }`, ideally paired with `replication.numAnalyticsNodes` so the example matches the recommendation.

evidence.quote: biConnector?: MongoDbBiConnector;

positiveFindings: The draft includes exactly one `<ApiReference definitionName="MongoDbAtlasClusterProps" />`, matching the page metadata.
All Stacktape configuration examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab and class-based `defineConfig` examples; there are no hand-authored YAML config examples.

The draft covers every primary MongoDbAtlasClusterProps top-level property at least once: `diskSizeGB`, `clusterTier`, `version`, `numShards`, `replication`, `enableBackups`, `enablePointInTimeRecovery`, `biConnector`, `autoScaling`, and `adminUserCredentials`.
The examples in Scaling, Replication, Backups, Sharding, Admin credentials, and Connecting to other resources are demonstrative: each sets the property discussed by the surrounding section.
The page correctly includes the pricing context from the source near the top and distinguishes shared M2/M5 tiers from dedicated M10+ tiers.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with TypeScript — no forbidden YAML or plain-object patterns anywhere on the page
Strong 'When to use' / 'When NOT to use' decision support with concrete alternatives (relational DB, DynamoDB) and honest caveats about setup overhead
All cluster tier variants (shared M2/M5, dedicated M10+, Low-CPU, NVMe) are explicitly called out — no single-variant tunnel vision
FeatureComparisonTable provides an at-a-glance breakdown of shared vs dedicated capabilities with cost anchors ($9/month, $57/month)

Technical accuracy is high throughout — property names, defaults, and constraints match the source types exactly (e.g., numElectableNodes: 3|5|7, PITR requires enableBackups + M10+, auto-scaling thresholds)
Admin credentials section cleanly distinguishes local/tool access from application access via connectTo — a common confusion point removed proactively
connectTo section includes a realistic MongoDB driver code snippet so a first-time user knows exactly how to consume the injected env var
Comprehensive FAQ with 10 entries covering all three required category types: Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, and comparison/choosing questions
problems: Provider configuration section is fatally thin for a first-time user: it says 'configure your Atlas API credentials in providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas' but gives zero guidance on what fields that object requires (API public key, private key, project ID, etc.). It then redirects to the API Reference, which covers MongoDbAtlasClusterProps only — not MongoDbAtlasProvider. A new user hitting this section cannot proceed to deploy.

The Warning 'Shared tiers (M2/M5) cannot use vpc or scoping-workloads-in-vpc access modes' is not grounded in the provided source files. The MongoDbAtlasAccessibility interface has no tier restrictions documented. Stating this as a hard constraint without source backing risks a source-grounding publication block.

The claim 'For Lambda functions, this means setting joinDefaultVpc: true on the function' in the Networking section is not present in the provided source files. It may be accurate, but it is asserted without grounding.

<FeatureComparisonTable> is not listed as a standard MDX component in the style guide or the reviewer surface. Its prop API (columns array, features array with nested values object) may not match what the docs system actually renders.
optionalImprovements: Expand the Provider configuration section with the actual fields of MongoDbAtlasProvider (publicApiKey, privateApiKey, orgId or projectId) so a first-time user can complete setup without leaving the page. If those fields are documented on a dedicated provider-config page, link to it explicitly ('See Provider configuration for Atlas API key setup') instead of pointing to the resource API Reference which doesn't cover it.

Either remove the shared-tier VPC restriction Warning or qualify it as 'a MongoDB Atlas platform limitation — see the Atlas documentation' rather than a Stacktape source-grounded constraint. Same for the joinDefaultVpc: true Lambda claim — either source it or soften to 'Lambda functions need VPC access to reach the cluster; see the Lambda function docs for VPC configuration'.
If FeatureComparisonTable is not a confirmed docs-system component, replace it with a standard markdown table to guarantee it renders correctly in production.

Add a code example showing a VPC or scoping-workloads-in-vpc accessibility configuration — the Networking section is the only major section without a config example, which breaks the consistent example-per-section pattern the rest of the page establishes.

Consider adding a brief sentence in the Replication section noting that each read-only and analytics node is a separate Atlas instance that adds to cluster cost, since this is a non-obvious tradeoff a production user would care about.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Comprehensive cluster tier coverage with a FeatureComparisonTable that clearly separates shared vs dedicated capabilities — a production engineer can make a tier decision without leaving the page.
All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with defineConfig and class-based resources. No YAML config blocks or StacktapeConfig type patterns appear.
Decision support is strong in nearly every section: when to enable auto-scaling, when to add read-only nodes, when sharding is and isn't needed — each section lands with an opinion.
FAQ mixes all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS/Atlas behavior, comparison questions) with 10 substantive Q&As, none of which are padded.
Env var naming is correct and consistent: resource named mainDb → STP_MAIN_DB_CONNECTION_STRING matches the STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM] pattern.
Pricing numbers ($9/month M2, $57/month M10) are sourced directly from the JSDoc and accurately reflected in the intro and FAQ.
Auto-scaling thresholds (75% up / 50% down) and disk expansion trigger (90%) are sourced from the type JSDoc and included verbatim — exactly what production engineers need.
Admin credentials section correctly distinguishes direct-access credentials from application connectTo access, preventing a common misconfiguration.
problems: CRITICAL CONTENT GAP — Networking section: The MongoDbAtlasAccessibility type (accessibilityMode, whitelistedIps) is defined in the source file but does NOT appear as a property on MongoDbAtlasClusterProps. The page describes 4 VPC access modes in a detailed table but provides no code example and the actual config path is ambiguous. The opening of the Provider Configuration section says 'provider config controls network accessibility', but the Networking section then treats it as if it's cluster-level. A production engineer trying to configure VPC-only access has no actionable path from this page.

Networking section: The claim 'Shared tiers (M2/M5) cannot use vpc or scoping-workloads-in-vpc access modes' is not stated anywhere in the provided source type definitions. MongoDbAtlasAccessibility has no tier restriction in the types. This may be a real Atlas platform limitation, but it reads as a Stacktape-enforced constraint and is not grounded in the provided source.

Lambda joinDefaultVpc: true claim: 'For Lambda functions, this means setting joinDefaultVpc: true' — this property is not in the MongoDB Atlas source files. It may be accurate at the Lambda type level, but making this assertion in the networking section of a MongoDB Atlas page without sourcing from Lambda types is a precision gap that could mislead engineers who have non-Lambda workloads.

BI Connector section describes readPreference options and performance tradeoffs but contains no code example, while every other major feature section (scaling, replication, backups, sharding) provides a working example. Inconsistent depth.
mandatoryFixes: Add a code example to the Networking section showing how to actually configure accessibilityMode (e.g., { accessibilityMode: 'vpc', whitelistedIps: ['...'] }). First verify and document the correct property path from MongoDbAtlasClusterProps or providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas — the source defines MongoDbAtlasAccessibility but does not attach it to MongoDbAtlasClusterProps, so the current prose is unactionable. If accessibility is a provider-level setting, the code example should reflect that; if it's per-cluster, show it in the cluster definition.

Remove or source-ground the shared-tier VPC restriction. The text 'Shared tiers (M2/M5) cannot use vpc or scoping-workloads-in-vpc access modes' is not grounded in the provided MongoDbAtlasAccessibility type or any other provided source. Either remove the Warning callout or rephrase to attribute this to MongoDB Atlas platform behavior ('MongoDB Atlas does not support VPC peering for M2/M5 clusters') rather than Stacktape's type constraints.

Add a code example to the BI Connector section. At minimum show biConnector: { enabled: true, readPreference: 'analytics' } inside a full defineConfig block. Every other opt-in feature section on this page has an example; omitting one here is inconsistent and leaves the BI Connector section as configure-without-guidance.
optionalImprovements: Clarify the Provider Configuration section: currently it says the provider config 'controls network accessibility' but the Networking section then describes modes as if they're cluster-level properties. One sentence explicitly stating whether accessibilityMode is on the cluster resource or in providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas would resolve this ambiguity.

Note the autoScaling bounds differ slightly from clusterTier: M400 (plain) is a valid clusterTier but does not appear in minClusterTier/maxClusterTier (only M400 Low-CPU and M400_NVME do). Engineers setting up auto-scaling on M400 should know the bounds list is not a 1-to-1 match.

The Provider Configuration section currently has no code example and no drill-down to show what mongoDbAtlas provider credentials look like. Even a one-line snippet referencing $Secret() for API credentials would match the level of detail in every other section.

The Lambda joinDefaultVpc: true claim in the Networking section should either be removed or linked directly to the Lambda function page where it can be verified — asserting a Lambda-specific config detail in a MongoDB Atlas page without a source reference creates a maintenance risk if that behavior changes.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and includes source-backed pricing context for M2 and M10 tiers.
The page uses TypeScript class-based Stacktape config examples inside <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/> and avoids forbidden hand-authored Stacktape YAML.
Sections are mostly short, scannable, and mapped to real user concerns: tiers, scaling, replication, backups, networking, sharding, BI Connector, and connection strings.
The cluster tier comparison table is useful and citation-friendly, and the FAQ covers Stacktape-specific, AWS/service-adjacent, and choosing/comparison questions.
problems: Several technical claims go beyond the provided source files. Examples: MongoDB Atlas API credential shape, BI tools such as Tableau/Power BI/Excel, MongoDB Compass/mongosh usage, rich queries/text search/geospatial comparisons, event logs/analytics use cases, and 'current stable release' for MongoDB 7.0 are not grounded in the supplied types.

The provider configuration section says credentials should be configured in providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas but does not show a copyable config example. That leaves a critical setup step vague, especially because MongoDB Atlas is the only database page here that requires providerConfig.

The draft claims the API Reference has the full list of provider configuration fields, but <ApiReference definitionName="MongoDbAtlasClusterProps" /> will only document MongoDbAtlasClusterProps, not root providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas fields.

The page says Stacktape grants a consuming workload 'temporary credential-less access' and injects the connection string. That phrase is source-backed for connectTo, but the page should avoid implying all local/admin/client access is credentialless; adminUserCredentials is explicitly separate.

Some feature availability wording is broader than the source. 'Custom replication is only available on dedicated tiers (M10+)' and 'dedicated tiers provide custom replication' are plausible from context but not directly stated in MongoDbReplication source comments.

The networking section introduces MongoDbAtlasAccessibility but does not show where providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas or accessibility is configured. Since accessibility is not nested under MongoDbAtlasClusterProps in the provided source, the page should be more cautious and avoid implying it is a cluster property.

Some specific recommendations are unsupported or overly precise, such as 'read-to-write ratio is high (10:1 or more)', 'M10 or M20 for small production workloads', and 'M30+ for production BI workloads'.

The examples set tunable values like version: '7.0', maxClusterTier: 'M40', numElectableNodes: 5, numReadOnlyNodes: 2, numAnalyticsNodes: 1, and numShards: 3. Most are partially explained, but maxClusterTier alternatives should note the supported auto-scaling tiers are a narrower M10+ union and not identical to the full clusterTier union.
mandatoryFixes: Correct the Provider configuration section: do not claim <ApiReference definitionName="MongoDbAtlasClusterProps" /> documents providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas fields. Either add a grounded root-config example if MongoDbAtlasProvider source is available, or state narrowly that providerConfig.mongoDbAtlas is required by the root StacktapeConfig source and link to the relevant provider configuration page.

Remove or qualify unsupported concrete claims that are not in the provided source files: MongoDB 7.0 as 'current stable release', BI tool examples, MongoDB Compass/mongosh examples, '10:1 or more', 'M10 or M20 for small production', 'M30+ for production BI workloads', and rich-query/text-search/geospatial comparisons unless supporting source is added.

Clarify networking placement. MongoDbAtlasAccessibility appears in the source, but not as a MongoDbAtlasClusterProps field in the provided files. Rewrite the section to say the provider/accessibility layer supports these modes only if the relevant provider config source confirms where the property is configured, or add the missing source.
Replace the unsupported statement that the API Reference contains provider configuration fields with a source-grounded statement about MongoDbAtlasClusterProps only.
Re-read every Stacktape <CodeBlock intellisense> after revisions to preserve defineConfig, class imports, TypeScript tab labels, and balanced focus markers.
optionalImprovements: Add a small providerConfig example if the MongoDbAtlasProvider type source can be supplied; this would make the page much more actionable.
Add one sentence near Basic example explaining that M10 is chosen because it is the lowest dedicated tier shown in the source, while M2/M5 are shared lower-cost tiers.
Move the 'providerConfig required' warning closer to the opening or Basic example so readers do not copy the resource example and discover the missing prerequisite later.
Tighten the FAQ answers by linking to the relevant page at the end of more answers, especially connecting resources, relational database, DynamoDB, bastion host, and referenceable parameters.

Consider adding a short 'Security model' subsection that separates connectTo application access, adminUserCredentials direct access, whitelisted IPs, and VPC/scoped access without over-claiming implementation details.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly grounded in the provided MongoDB Atlas type definitions and helper JSDoc. The main factual risks are a few over-specific operational claims not present in source, one invalid/invented internal anchor link, and one broad claim about Stacktape database resources that is contradicted by providerConfig source.

issues.statement: “These credentials let Stacktape provision and manage Atlas resources on your behalf.” plus the Info link “[Provider configuration](#provider-configuration)”.
issues.reason: The link target is an MDX heading anchor, not a route in the provided documentation navigation index. The verifier instructions classify cross-links outside the navigation index as invented routes. Anchor links are not listed in the navigation index, so this should be removed or replaced with plain text unless the docs system explicitly allows same-page anchors.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the markdown link and write “Configure Atlas API credentials in the provider configuration section below.”

issues.statement: “This is the only Stacktape database resource that requires external provider credentials — AWS-native databases like relational databases and DynamoDB work without additional accounts.”
issues.reason: The source shows `providerConfig` is required for both `mongo-db-atlas-cluster` and `upstash-redis`. Upstash Redis is in the Databases navigation section, so MongoDB Atlas is not the only database resource requiring external provider credentials.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: “MongoDB Atlas requires external provider credentials. AWS-native databases such as relational databases and DynamoDB do not require a separate provider account; Upstash Redis also uses provider configuration.”

evidence.quote: Credentials and settings for 3rd-party services (MongoDB Atlas, Upstash).
Required only if you use `mongo-db-atlas-cluster` or `upstash-redis` resources in your stack.

issues.statement: “The `version` property defaults to `"7.0"` — MongoDB's current stable release.” and FAQ: “The default is 7.0, which is MongoDB's current stable release.”
issues.reason: The Stacktape source supports the default and allowed values, but it does not support the claim that MongoDB 7.0 is MongoDB's current stable release. That assertion is also time-sensitive and unnecessary for configuring Stacktape.
issues.suggestedFix: Say: “The `version` property defaults to `"7.0"`. Supported values are `5.0`, `6.0`, and `7.0`.”

evidence.quote:
MongoDB engine version.
   * @default "7.0"

issues.statement: “Read-only nodes (`numReadOnlyNodes`) — replica nodes that scale read throughput but can never become primary.”
issues.reason: The source supports that read-only nodes scale read throughput, but it does not state that they can never become primary. That may be true for MongoDB Atlas generally, but it is not grounded in the provided Stacktape source.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow to: “Read-only nodes (`numReadOnlyNodes`) add replica nodes for scaling read throughput.”

evidence.quote: Read-only replica nodes for scaling read throughput.

issues.statement: “Shared-tier regional availability may be limited — check the Atlas documentation for supported regions.”
issues.reason: The provided source documents shared tiers and dedicated tiers but does not mention regional availability constraints. This is not a Stacktape-supported constraint in the provided source set.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the sentence or rephrase as general non-Stacktape guidance only if external Atlas docs are part of the source set.

evidence.quote: Instance size. M2/M5 = shared (cheapest). M10+ = dedicated (more features, auto-scaling, backups).

issues.statement: “Higher tiers (M40+) include specialized variants.”
issues.reason: The clusterTier union includes Low-CPU and NVMe variants for M40, M50, M60, M80, M200, M300, M400, and M700, but not for M100 or M140. “M40+” implies every higher tier has specialized variants, which overstates the source.
issues.suggestedFix: Say: “Several higher tiers include specialized Low-CPU or NVMe variants, such as M40, M50, M60, M80, M200, M300, M400, and M700.”

issues.statement: “For Lambda functions, this means setting `joinDefaultVpc: true` on the function.”
issues.reason: The claim may be true in the broader Stacktape type surface, but no LambdaFunctionProps source is included in this page source set. The MongoDB source only says `scoping-workloads-in-vpc` requires security-group access via `connectTo`, not how Lambda joins the VPC.
issues.suggestedFix: Either include the Lambda source in the page source set or narrow to: “For workloads that are not already in the VPC, configure the workload’s VPC access according to its resource page.”

issues.statement: “Set `adminUserCredentials` with a username and password to create an admin user for direct access.”
issues.reason: The source says `adminUserCredentials` is an admin user for direct database access, but it does not explicitly say Stacktape creates that user. The wording is likely intended but slightly stronger than the source.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow to: “Set `adminUserCredentials` to configure an admin user for direct database access.”

positiveFindings: The page uses class-based `defineConfig` examples in `<CodeBlock intellisense>` and does not hand-author Stacktape YAML.
The `<ApiReference definitionName="MongoDbAtlasClusterProps" />` value matches the resource props type in the source.
The pricing context near the top is grounded in the MongoDB Atlas cluster JSDoc.
The connectTo environment variable pattern and the concrete `STP_MAIN_DB_CONNECTION_STRING` example match the resource name and helper JSDoc.
The sections on cluster tiers, auto-scaling, backups, PITR, sharding, BI Connector, and referenceable parameters generally align with the provided type definitions.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the provided MongoDB Atlas type definitions, but several Stacktape-specific constraints and setup claims are either contradicted by the source or not grounded in the supplied files. The biggest problem is the claim that MongoDB Atlas is the only database resource requiring external provider credentials, which contradicts the root config source.

issues.statement: “This is the only Stacktape database resource that requires external provider credentials — AWS-native databases like relational databases and DynamoDB work without additional accounts.”
issues.reason: The root Stacktape config source says third-party provider config is required for both MongoDB Atlas and Upstash Redis resources. Upstash Redis is also listed as a database page in the navigation, so the “only” claim is wrong.
issues.suggestedFix: Write: “MongoDB Atlas requires third-party provider credentials. Stacktape’s AWS-native databases, such as relational databases and DynamoDB, do not require an additional provider account; Upstash Redis also uses providerConfig because it is a third-party provider.”

evidence.quote:
Credentials and settings for 3rd-party services (MongoDB Atlas, Upstash).

Required only if you use `mongo-db-atlas-cluster` or `upstash-redis` resources in your stack.

issues.statement: “Shared tiers (M2/M5) cannot use `vpc` or `scoping-workloads-in-vpc` access modes. If you use shared tiers, the accessibility mode must be `internet` or `whitelisted-ips-only`.”
issues.reason: The provided MongoDB Atlas source defines the four accessibility modes but does not state any tier restriction for `vpc` or `scoping-workloads-in-vpc`. The source does explicitly call out tier restrictions on other properties, such as disk size and sharding, so this hard networking constraint needs validator/runtime evidence before being documented.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the shared-tier networking restriction, or narrow it to: “The source defines four accessibility modes; check Atlas and Stacktape validation for tier-specific networking availability before relying on VPC-only access for shared tiers.”

evidence.quote:
- **`internet`**: Accessible from anywhere (credentials still required).
   * - **`vpc`**: Only from resources in your VPC + any `whitelistedIps`.
   * - **`scoping-workloads-in-vpc`**: Like `vpc`, but also requires security-group access via `connectTo`.
   * - **`whitelisted-ips-only`**: Only from IP addresses listed in `whitelistedIps`.
Disk size in GB. Not available for shared tiers (M2/M5). M10+ auto-scales storage by default.

issues.statement: “When using `vpc` or `scoping-workloads-in-vpc` mode, workloads that connect to the cluster must be able to reach the VPC. For Lambda functions, this means setting `joinDefaultVpc: true` on the function.”
issues.reason: The MongoDB Atlas source only defines the VPC accessibility modes. The supplied files do not include a Lambda property named `joinDefaultVpc` or a MongoDB Atlas rule requiring that property for Lambda access.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the grounded VPC access statement and remove the Lambda-specific property claim unless the Lambda source for this page is added. For example: “For VPC-scoped access, the consuming workload must run where it can reach the VPC-protected Atlas endpoint.”

evidence.quote:
- **`vpc`**: Only from resources in your VPC + any `whitelistedIps`.
   * - **`scoping-workloads-in-vpc`**: Like `vpc`, but also requires security-group access via `connectTo`.

issues.statement: “The `version` property defaults to `"7.0"` — MongoDB's current stable release.” Also repeated in the FAQ: “The default is 7.0, which is MongoDB's current stable release.”
issues.reason: The Stacktape source supports the default and allowed values, but it does not support the claim that MongoDB 7.0 is the current stable MongoDB release. That “current” claim is time-sensitive and unnecessary for the Stacktape behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Write: “The `version` property defaults to `"7.0"`. Supported versions are `5.0`, `6.0`, and `7.0`.”

evidence.quote:
MongoDB engine version.
   * @default "7.0"
   */
  version?: '5.0' | '6.0' | '7.0';

issues.statement: “Add read-only nodes when your read-to-write ratio is high (10:1 or more) and you're seeing latency on the primary.”
issues.reason: The source says read-only replica nodes scale read throughput, but it does not provide a 10:1 threshold. The numeric threshold reads like a documented rule of thumb but is not grounded in the supplied source.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the numeric threshold: “Add read-only nodes when read traffic is high enough that primary query latency becomes a bottleneck.”

evidence.quote: Read-only replica nodes for scaling read throughput.

positiveFindings: The page uses `defineConfig` with class-based Stacktape config examples inside `<CodeBlock intellisense>` and does not hand-author YAML examples.
`<ApiReference definitionName="MongoDbAtlasClusterProps" />` matches the resource props type in the provided source.
The pricing context for M2 and M10 is grounded in the MongoDB Atlas cluster JSDoc.

The documented `connectTo` environment variable pattern and concrete `STP_MAIN_DB_CONNECTION_STRING` example match the resource-name-dependent convention and the `MongoDbAtlasCluster` injected parameter source.

The sharding, backup, PITR, auto-scaling thresholds, disk scaling threshold, BI Connector read preferences, and referenceable `connectionString` parameter are supported by the supplied type definitions.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the main MongoDbAtlasClusterProps surface well: clusterTier, version, scaling, replication, backups, PITR, sharding, BI Connector, admin credentials, referenceable params, and connectTo. The main API-completeness gap is that the Networking section explains provider-level access modes but does not include a demonstrative Stacktape config example for the access-mode property it documents. The main union-coverage gap is the large clusterTier enum being summarized rather than explicitly named.

issues.statement: The `clusterTier` property determines the instance size, cost, and available features. Supported values range from shared tiers (`M2`, `M5`) to dedicated tiers (`M10` through `M700`), including Low-CPU and NVMe variants for specialized workloads.
issues.reason: The draft documents a union-typed property but does not name all supported tier literals. The source defines exact allowed values, including specific Low-CPU and NVMe names. Summarizing as `M10 through M700` can imply every intermediate tier or variant is accepted, while the API only accepts the listed string literals.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a compact table or sentence that names the exact supported clusterTier values, or say the full literal list is in the API reference and avoid implying continuous range support. For example: `Supported dedicated tiers are M10, M20, M30, M40, M50, M60, M80, M100, M140, M200, M300, M400, and M700, plus the Low-CPU and NVMe variants listed in the API reference.`

issues.statement: ## Networking documents `internet`, `vpc`, `scoping-workloads-in-vpc`, `whitelisted-ips-only`, and `whitelistedIps`, but provides no Stacktape config example showing where those settings are configured.
issues.reason: The section documents a union-typed access-mode feature and gives important operational guidance, but the code-example demonstrativeness check expects a section that explains a configurable feature to show the actual property being set. Readers cannot see whether `accessibilityMode` belongs on the cluster, provider config, or another nested object.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a focused `<CodeBlock intellisense>` example in the Networking section that sets the actual provider/networking property path for MongoDB Atlas accessibility, using one non-default mode such as `whitelisted-ips-only` or `vpc`. Keep the example grounded in the available config type path; if the exact path is not in the provided source, narrow the prose and defer the exact shape to the API reference instead of implying it.

evidence.quote:
## Networking

MongoDB Atlas network access is represented by the `MongoDbAtlasAccessibility` configuration with four access modes:

interface MongoDbAtlasAccessibility {
  /**
   * #### Network access mode.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * - **`internet`**: Accessible from anywhere (credentials still required).
   * - **`vpc`**: Only from resources in your VPC + any `whitelistedIps`.
   * - **`scoping-workloads-in-vpc`**: Like `vpc`, but also requires security-group access via `connectTo`.
   * - **`whitelisted-ips-only`**: Only from IP addresses listed in `whitelistedIps`.
   *
   * @default internet
   */
  accessibilityMode: 'internet' | 'vpc' | 'scoping-workloads-in-vpc' | 'whitelisted-ips-only';

positiveFindings: The draft includes exactly one `<ApiReference definitionName="MongoDbAtlasClusterProps" />`, matching the page metadata typeName.

Every Stacktape configuration example uses `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `label: 'TypeScript'` tab and class-based `defineConfig` style; no hand-authored YAML Stacktape config appears in the draft.

The major primary properties on `MongoDbAtlasClusterProps` are covered in prose or examples: `clusterTier`, `version`, `diskSizeGB`, `numShards`, `replication`, `enableBackups`, `enablePointInTimeRecovery`, `biConnector`, `autoScaling`, and `adminUserCredentials`.

Most feature sections have demonstrative examples: Scaling sets `autoScaling`, Replication sets `replication`, Backups sets `enableBackups` and `enablePointInTimeRecovery`, Sharding sets `numShards`, and Admin credentials sets `adminUserCredentials`.
